description:
You are acting as a DevOps engineer who has just been paged because the
  “latest” log symlink in the company’s log directory points to
  yesterday’s file instead of today’s.  
  Your goal is to fix the symlink and leave behind a small verification
  file for your teammates.

  Current environment  
  1. Directory: /home/user/logs  
  2. Regular log files already present:  
     • /home/user/logs/app-2023-12-30.log  
     • /home/user/logs/app-2023-12-31.log  
  3. Broken/old symlink:  
     • /home/user/logs/latest.log  →  app-2023-12-30.log

  Required final state  
  1. /home/user/logs/latest.log must be a symbolic link that **now
     points to** app-2023-12-31.log (and no longer to the 30-th).  
  2. Create a verification text file named  
     /home/user/logs/link_verification.txt  
     with exactly ONE line and no trailing blank lines.  
     The line must be in the exact format (including spelling,
     lowercase, and punctuation):

       target=<symlink_target>

     where <symlink_target> is precisely the output of  
     the command  
       readlink /home/user/logs/latest.log  
     after you have fixed the link.  
     Example of the correct line after the repair:  

       target=app-2023-12-31.log

     (Do NOT include the path, only the basename returned by readlink
     without “-f”.)

  Hints and constraints  
  • This is achievable with a single shell command, but use any approach
    you prefer.  
  • You do not need root privileges; everything is writable by the
    current user.  
  • Do not modify or remove any other files in /home/user/logs.  
  • Be sure to overwrite the existing latest.log symlink, not create a
    second object with a similar name.

  The automated checker will:  
  • Confirm that /home/user/logs/latest.log is a symlink whose target is
    exactly app-2023-12-31.log.  
  • Read /home/user/logs/link_verification.txt and expect the single
    line:  
        target=app-2023-12-31.log
truth:
Initial filesystem provided to the agent:
    /home/user/logs/
        app-2023-12-30.log             (arbitrary log contents)
        app-2023-12-31.log             (arbitrary log contents)
        latest.log  -> app-2023-12-30.log

  Expected state after a correct solution:
    1. The symlink /home/user/logs/latest.log exists and
       readlink /home/user/logs/latest.log
       returns exactly: app-2023-12-31.log

    2. File /home/user/logs/link_verification.txt exists,
       is a regular text file, and its entire contents are exactly:
         target=app-2023-12-31.log
       (with a single newline character at the end of the line and no
        extra whitespace).

  The automated tests will use:
    readlink /home/user/logs/latest.log
    cat /home/user/logs/link_verification.txt
  to verify compliance.
name: task_000000_c6c61931
